source.title: Diccionario Quechua-Español-Quechua, Qheswa-Español-Qheswa, Simi Taqe
source.author: Academia Mayor de la Lengua Quechua de Cusco (AMLQ)

metadata.target_application: Spanish->Chanka (Ayacucho, quy_Latn) translation grounding lookup. The Chanka parallel corpus has a judicial/administrative domain bias, so judicial terms are prioritized.

extraction.method: pdfplumber column-aware text extraction of pages 402-479 (Spanish->Quechua half of the dictionary); line-aware entry-head regex; manual curation by frequency / domain.
selection_pipeline: Phase 1: lemmas from a hand-curated COMMON_WORDS list (~330 entries: function words, body parts, kinship, numbers, basic verbs/adjectives, time, common nouns).
Phase 2: lemmas from a hand-curated JUDICIAL_DOMAIN_KEYWORDS list (~150 entries: judicial/administrative Spanish from the Chanka source corpus domain).

Phase 3: backfill with the shortest single-word lowercase lemmas in the parsed dictionary (which tend to be the most basic / most reused), excluding entries whose 'Quechua' equals their Spanish (loanwords or stub entries).
extraction.alias_handling: A few compound entries in the source (e.g. 'hijo del padre' -> Churi) were aliased to their bare lemma ('hijo'). Aliased entries are marked in their raw text.

caveats: Source dictionary is Cuzco Quechua (quz_Latn). Chanka (Ayacucho, quy_Latn) shares the bulk of lexicon but is NOT identical.

Cuzco has ejective consonants (k', t', p', q', ch') and aspirates (kh, th, ph, qh, chh). Chanka does NOT distinguish these from plain stops. Where Cuzco writes 'k'aspi' Chanka writes plain 'kaspi'. The chanka_note field flags any Cuzco-only feature found and gives a naive Chanka-orthography suggestion produced by stripping the ejective apostrophe / aspirate <h>.

Vowel allophony: Cuzco often writes <e o> next to /q/ (e.g. 'qhepa', 'qosa'). Chanka typically also writes <e o> in those positions in the official Ayacucho orthography (e.g. 'qipa', 'qusa' is the academic 3-vowel form, but field varieties match Cuzco). Vowel restoration is NOT attempted; trust this lookup for CONSONANTS, double-check vowels against an Ayacucho/Chanka primary source for high-stakes use.
Cuzco 'h' (e.g. 'hatun', 'hamuy') is the same in Chanka. Cuzco 'w' is the same in Chanka.

Some Cuzco lexemes have no Chanka cognate even when orthographically clean. 'shared root' in chanka_note ONLY means there are no Cuzco-only ejectives/aspirates to strip; it does NOT certify that the word is attested in Chanka.

Trust this lookup for ROOTS / LEMMAS, NOT for inflected forms. Quechua is highly agglutinative; surface forms in the Chanka corpus will carry suffixes (-ta, -kuna, -man, -manta, -pi, -wan, -paq, -y, -ki, -n, -nchik, -niku, -nku, -chka-, -rqa-, -saq, -sun, etc.) that are not encoded here.

Some entries' qu_cuzco field may still contain a leading domain-tag fragment (e.g. 'Astron. P'unchay' or 'Anat. wiksa') because the source typography mixed those into the headword body; the actual Quechua root is the last capitalized token.

The dictionary marks regional variants with labels Pe.Aya (Ayacucho/Chanka), Pe.Anc (Ancash), Pe.Caj (Cajamarca), Pe.Jun (Junin), Pe.S.Mar (San Martin), Arg (Santiago del Estero), Bol (Bolivia), Ec (Ecuador). When present in the source, Pe.Aya IS the citation-quality Chanka form. We did NOT systematically extract Pe.Aya forms into this lookup -- they live in the raw source on the cited page. For high-stakes verification, re-read the source entry.
Page numbers refer to the PDF page INDEX (1-based) inside the source file, not the printed page number. The Spanish->Quechua half runs roughly from PDF page 402 to 479.
Some headwords in the source are gendered: 'abogado,–da' -> abogado/abogada. We strip the gender suffix and keep the masculine form as the lemma. Both genders share the same Quechua translation.

schema.es: Spanish lemma (headword), lowercase. May be a multi-word compound (e.g. 'hijo del padre').
schema.qu_cuzco: Primary Cuzco Quechua translation (first listed in source).
schema.qu_cuzco_synonyms: Additional Cuzco synonyms from the same entry (same gloss, alternative roots).
schema.pos: Part-of-speech abbreviation from source (s=sustantivo/noun, v=verb, adj=adjective, adv=adverb, pron=pronoun, prep=preposition, interj=interjection, conj=conjunction, p=participle, num=numeral, loc=locution).
schema.gloss: Spanish gloss; currently same as es; for richer gloss inspect the source page.
schema.chanka_note: Either 'shared root (no Cuzco-only ejectives/aspirates)' or 'Cuzco-only feature: <word> (<feature list>) -> Chanka likely <stripped form>'.
schema.page: PDF page INDEX (1-based) where the entry appears in the source PDF.

recommended_use: USE this lookup to ground a Spanish word in a verified Cuzco Quechua root before letting a reasoning model speculate about morpheme shape.
USE the chanka_note to decide whether the Chanka surface form should differ orthographically from the Cuzco citation form.
DO NOT use this lookup to confirm that an inflected Chanka surface form is correct; that requires morphology rules.
DO NOT treat absence of a lemma as evidence that the word doesn't exist in Quechua -- the curation is a deliberate subset of ~700 of the dictionary's ~5400 ES->QU entries.
